1: yĕsûdātô bĕharrê qōdesh — his foundation is on the holy mountains. The subject (yĕsûdātô: his foundation, that which he has founded) is initially ambiguous — whose foundation? Context makes it Zion/Jerusalem. Harrê qōdesh (holy mountains — plural) may include all the ridges of the Jerusalem hill system, or may be a superlative plural for the supremely holy mountain. The foundation metaphor establishes permanence: what is founded on mountains does not shift.
2: ʾōhēb YHWH shaʿărê ṣiyyôn mikkol mishkĕnôt yaʿăqōb — the LORD loves the gates of Zion more than all the dwelling places of Jacob. ʾĀhēb (loves — the divine love as the basis for Zion's election, as in Psalms 78:68). Shaʿărê ṣiyyôn (gates of Zion — the gates as the most public, civic part of the city; what YHWH loves is not just the sanctuary but the whole city in its public life). The comparison with all the other dwelling places of Jacob makes the preference explicit and total.
3: nĕkhabbādôt mĕdubbār bākh ʿîr hāʾĕlōhîm selāh — glorious things are spoken of you, O city of God. Nĕkhabbādôt (glorious things — niphal participle of kābad: weighty, honored things, things befitting the divine kābôd) — the oracle about Zion carries the weight of divine glory. ʿÎr hāʾĕlōhîm (city of God) — the title that makes Jerusalem not merely a capital but a theological entity. The selah pause before the astonishing v4.
4: ʾazkîr rahab ûbābel lĕyōdĕʿāy — among those who acknowledge me I will name Rahab and Babylon. Rahab (the mythological chaos dragon, used as a poetic name for Egypt, cf. Psalms 89:10; Isaiah 30:7; Psalms 51:9) — Egypt and Babylon, the two great enemies of Israel's history, are named in YHWH's register. hinnēh pĕleshet wĕṣōr ʿim-kûsh zeh yullad-shām — behold — Philistia, Tyre, and Ethiopia: this one was born there. Five nations (Egypt, Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, Ethiopia) representing the known world — all its great powers — enrolled in Zion's birth register. Zeh yullad-shām (this one was born there) is the phrase inscribed next to each nation's name, granting them Zion-citizenship regardless of their geographic birth. The OT's boldest universalist vision: spiritual re-birth in Zion that transcends ethnic origin.
5: wĕlĕṣiyyôn yēʾāmar ʾîsh wĕʾîsh yullad bāh — of Zion it will be said, 'This one and that one were born in her'. The repeated birth formula (yullad bāh: born in her) applies now to every individual — not just the great nations but each person enrolled. wĕhûʾ yĕkhônĕnehā ʿelyôn — and the Most High himself will establish her. Yĕkhônēn (piel of kûn: to establish, make firm) — divine agency is the source of Zion's permanence; her stability is not political but theological, guaranteed by ʿelyôn (the Most High).
6: YHWH yispōr bikhtôb ʿammîm — when the LORD records the enrollment of the peoples. Sāphar (to count, to record, to write in a register) — the divine census-taking. Bikhtôb ʿammîm (in the writing/register of the peoples) — a heavenly population register in which the nations are enrolled. zeh yullad-shām selāh — this one was born there. The registration formula repeated — every enrolled person has the same notation: Zion-born. The selah marks the astonishment of the vision.
7: wĕshārîm kĕḥōlĕlîm kol-maʿyānay bākh — singers and dancers alike say, 'All my springs of life are in you'. Maʿyān (spring, source of water — used of living water sources in Song of Solomon 4:15; Psalms 84:6) — Zion as the source of all life's vitality. The springs are plural (maʿyānay: my springs) but all in Zion — all the sources of life converge in the city of God. The psalm closes not with a theological statement but with a personal affirmation of Zion as the totality of one's life-source.
